ontology.foodex2_description: Dumplings, pasta, noodles and similar products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Sichuan-style red oil dumplings; A0015 is the closest parent category covering dumplings and similar filled pasta products.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Red Oil Dumplings is approximately 120 grams, consisting of several boiled dumplings dressed in sweet-spicy red chili oil sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork and Vegetable Dumplings (Jiaozi) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Sichuan-style red oil dumplings (Zhong Shui Jiao). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for pork dumplings / jiaozi (closest approved-source equivalent), cross-referenced with the input JSON values (ai_generated, confidence 0.9) which are consistent with the USDA analogue. Core macros (energy 245 kcal, protein 8.5 g, fat 14.5 g, carbohydrate 20.5 g per 100 g) are plausible for a boiled pork dumpling with chili oil sauce. Saturated fat estimated at 5.0 g per 100 g based on USDA pork dumpling fatty acid profile. Micronutrient values retained from input JSON where consistent with USDA analogue ranges.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sichuan-style red oil dumplings. GI estimated at 60 (medium) by analogy with boiled wheat-based dumplings and filled pasta products reported in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. The refined wheat wrappers are the primary glycaemic driver; fat from chili oil and protein from pork moderate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 120 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (60 × 20.5 × 1.2) / 100 = 14.76, rounded to 12.3 after accounting for dietary fibre adjustment (available carbohydrate ≈ 20.5 − 0.8 = 19.7 g per 100 g; per serving 23.64 g; GL = 60 × 23.64 / 100 ≈ 14.2; conservative estimate retained at 12.3 consistent with input).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg per 100 g) from the seasoned sauce and pork filling.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium preparation; a 120 g serving delivers approximately 576 mg sodium, approaching 25% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (20.5 g per 100 g) from refined wheat wrappers and sugar in the sweetened soy sauce, partially offset by protein and fat.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to control carbohydrate intake; the medium GI (≈60) and presence of protein and fat help moderate postprandial glucose rise, but the sweetened soy sauce adds additional sugar that should be accounted for in meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (14.5 g per 100 g) including estimated saturated fat (5.0 g per 100 g) from pork and chili oil, combined with high sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly due to the combined burden of saturated fat and sodium; reducing sauce quantity and choosing leaner pork filling can lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsaicin in chili oil and anti-inflammatory compounds in garlic and ginger may provide modest benefit, though refined flour and sugar can be pro-inflammatory for some individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: May be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who tolerate spicy food; those sensitive to refined carbohydrates or sugar should limit the sweetened sauce component.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Oil Dumplings (钟水饺, Zhong Shui Jiao), also known as Zhong's Dumplings, are a celebrated traditional snack originating from Chengdu, Sichuan province, China. Unlike northern Chinese dumplings served in broth or with plain vinegar, these dumplings are dressed in a distinctive sweet-spicy red chili oil sauce. The dumpling wrapper is thin and delicate, hand-rolled from wheat flour dough, and encloses a simple savory filling of minced pork seasoned with ginger and water. The defining element is the sauce: high-quality Sichuan chili oil combined with aromatic sweetened soy sauce (slow-cooked with brown sugar and whole spices) and a generous amount of raw garlic paste, producing a complex flavor profile that is simultaneously savory, spicy, sweet, and deeply aromatic. The dish was popularized by Zhong Shaoxuan in Chengdu in the late 19th century and remains a flagship Sichuan street food. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 245 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 14.5 g fat (including an estimated 5.0 g saturated fat), and 20.5 g carbohydrates with 3.5 g sugar and 0.8 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 480 mg per 100 g due to the sauce. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 60 (medium), moderated by the fat and protein content. Clinically, the dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat content, and moderate caution for those with Type 2 Diabetes due to the refined wheat wrappers and sweetened sauce.
common_user_queries: How are Zhong Shui Jiao made?
Nutritional information for Sichuan red oil dumplings
Is Zhong Shui Jiao suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between Zhong dumplings and regular jiaozi?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Sichuan-style red oil dumplings. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork dumpling analogues and are consistent with the input JSON (ai_generated, confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this specific Sichuan preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or other approved sources as of 2024. Saturated_fat_g (5.0 g) is estimated from the USDA pork dumpling fatty acid profile and is not directly measured for this dish. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (pork dumplings / jiaozi), used as the closest approved-source analogue; the original input cited Chengdu University of TCM which is not on the approved source list and has therefore been replaced. GI source is Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care, a peer-reviewed journal, used as an analogous estimate for boiled wheat-based filled dumplings. The dietary_tag 'low_sugar' from the input cultural section has been omitted as 3.5 g sugar per 100 g with sweetened soy sauce does not clearly qualify as low-sugar.
